edges.description: The aberrantly activated P250R receptor sustains downstream MAPK/ERK signaling in cranial suture osteoblasts.
edges.target: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

edges.description: Altered FGFR3 signaling also affects craniofacial and hand/foot skeletal development outside the fused coronal suture.
edges.target: FGFR3 craniofacial and appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance

edges.description: Altered dosage-sensitive FGF signaling affects auditory sensory epithelium development and function.
FGFR3 P250R is associated with variably penetrant developmental delay and intellectual disability through incompletely resolved neurodevelopmental mechanisms.
Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling accelerates osteogenic differentiation and premature suture fusion.
edges.source: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

edges.description: Premature coronal suture fusion manifests clinically as coronal craniosynostosis.
Craniofacial skeletal patterning disturbance manifests as midface retrusion.
edges.source: FGFR3 craniofacial and appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance

edges.description: Appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance manifests as brachydactyly.
edges.source: FGFR3 craniofacial and appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance

edges.description: Appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance manifests as carpal and/or tarsal fusion.
edges.source: FGFR3 craniofacial and appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance

edges.description: Inner-ear sensory epithelial dysfunction manifests as sensorineural hearing loss.
Variable neurodevelopmental involvement manifests as intellectual disability in a subset of affected individuals.

nodes.description: Short hands with brachydactyly may occur, though limb involvement is generally mild.

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: some patients had abnormalities on radiographs of hands and feet, including thimble-like middle phalanges, coned epiphyses, and carpal and tarsal fusions. Brachydactyly was seen in some cases

nodes.description: Fusion of carpal or tarsal bones may be seen on radiographs.

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: some patients had abnormalities on radiographs of hands and feet, including thimble-like middle phalanges, coned epiphyses, and carpal and tarsal fusions

nodes.description: Uni- or bilateral coronal suture fusion is the most common feature. Bilateral involvement causes brachycephaly; unilateral causes plagiocephaly.

evidence.reference_title: Muenke syndrome: An international multicenter natural history study.
evidence.snippet: Eighty-five percent of the participants had craniosynostosis (16 of 103 did not have craniosynostosis)

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: coronal synostosis is due to an amino acid substitution (Pro250Arg) that results from a single point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene

nodes.description: The P250R mutation in FGFR3 causes altered ligand binding specificity, allowing aberrant activation by non-cognate FGF ligands. In cranial sutures, this drives premature osteogenic differentiation and suture fusion; in other craniofacial, appendicular skeletal, inner-ear, and neurodevelopmental contexts it contributes to the variable extra-sutural phenotype of Muenke syndrome. The mechanism differs from achondroplasia-type mutations that predominantly affect growth plate chondrocytes.

evidence.reference_title: The molecular and genetic basis of fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 disorders: the achondroplasia family of skeletal dysplasias, Muenke craniosynostosis, and Crouzon syndrome with acanthosis nigricans.
evidence.snippet: Recent evidence suggests that the phenotypic differences may be due to specific alleles with varying degrees of ligand-independent activation, allowing the receptor to be constitutively active.

nodes.description: Extra-sutural skeletal effects of FGFR3 P250R include midface retrusion and hand/foot abnormalities such as brachydactyly and carpal or tarsal fusion.
nodes.id: FGFR3 craniofacial and appendicular skeletal patterning disturbance

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: middle phalanges, coned epiphyses, and carpal and tarsal fusions

nodes.description: Dosage-sensitive FGF signaling affects differentiation of auditory sensory epithelium; FGFR3 P250R produces predominantly low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in humans and a concordant cochlear epithelial phenotype in the mouse model.

evidence.reference_title: Hearing loss in a mouse model of Muenke syndrome.
Hearing loss in a mouse model of Muenke syndrome.

nodes.description: Developmental delay or intellectual disability occurs in a subset of patients, though many have normal intelligence.

evidence.reference_title: Muenke syndrome: An international multicenter natural history study.
evidence.snippet: developmental delay in 66.3%, intellectual disability in 35.6%

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: developmental delay was seen in a minority

nodes.description: Midface hypoplasia may be present but is typically milder than in Crouzon or Apert syndromes.
Premature osteogenic differentiation in the coronal suture causes early coronal suture fusion, the predominant craniosynostosis pattern of Muenke syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A unique point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene (FGFR3) defines a new craniosynostosis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Here we present 61 individuals from 20 unrelated families where coronal synostosis is due to an amino acid substitution (Pro250Arg) that results from a single point mutation in the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene on chromosome 4p.

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing loss is common and may be progressive. It is an important feature distinguishing Muenke from other craniosynostosis syndromes.

evidence.reference_title: Muenke syndrome: An international multicenter natural history study.
Hearing loss in a mouse model of Muenke syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The Muenke syndrome cohort showed significant, but incompletely penetrant, predominantly low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss

evidence.reference_title: Muenke syndrome (FGFR3-related craniosynostosis): expansion of the phenotype and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: a majority of the patients (95%) demonstrated a mild-to-moderate, low frequency sensorineural hearing loss

nodes.description: The P250R FGFR3 receptor, with its altered ligand specificity, drives sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in osteoblast-lineage cells of the cranial suture. This is the conserved effector branch shared with the other FGFR craniosynostoses; in FGFR3 the receptor shows accelerated, predominantly ligand-dependent ERK activation.
nodes.id: Sustained MAPK/ERK signaling in suture osteoblasts

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 mutations promote apoptosis but do not alter chondrocyte proliferation in thanatophoric dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Early ERKs activation following FGF 9 supplementation was observed in TD chondrocytes (2 min) as compared with controls (5 min) but no signal was detected in the absence of ligand.

nodes.description: Muenke syndrome includes variably penetrant developmental delay and intellectual disability, although the intermediate mechanisms linking FGFR3 P250R to cognition remain incompletely resolved.

evidence.reference_title: Muenke syndrome: An international multicenter natural history study.
